title: Standard algorithms

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Standard algorithms; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: Standard C++ Tour; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Standard algorithms
sources.claim: Standard algorithms express operations over iterator or range inputs with documented preconditions, complexity, mutation, and result semantics independent of one container type.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Standard algorithms express operations over iterator or range inputs with documented preconditions, complexity, mutation, and result semantics independent of one container type.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: sort orders a vector under a valid comparator, then lower_bound is used only while that sorted precondition remains true. Your goal is to replace a hand-written traversal with a clear standard algorithm and test it. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Iterators and invalidation; Overloading and default arguments. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Standard algorithms express operations over iterator or range inputs with documented preconditions, complexity, mutation, and result semantics independent of one container type. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Algorithms may require iterator categories, sorted input, valid output capacity, or nonoverlapping ranges, and remove-like algorithms do not erase container elements themselves. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: std::remove_if directly changes a vector's size and destroys every removed element.

includes: Use find, count, transform, remove-style and sorting algorithms over ranges.
Replace a hand-written traversal with a clear standard algorithm and test it.
excludes: Algorithms may require iterator categories, sorted input, valid output capacity, or nonoverlapping ranges, and remove-like algorithms do not erase container elements themselves.

outcomes: Replace a hand-written traversal with a clear standard algorithm and test it.

examples.title: Standard algorithms: defined case
examples.situation: sort orders a vector under a valid comparator, then lower_bound is used only while that sorted precondition remains true.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: sort orders a vector under a valid comparator, then lower_bound is used only while that sorted precondition remains true.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: remove_if rearranges and returns a new logical end; use the container's erase operation to reduce its size.
examples.result: The defined result follows because remove_if rearranges and returns a new logical end; use the container's erase operation to reduce its size.

examples.title: Standard algorithms: boundary case
examples.situation: remove_if compacts retained elements and returns a new logical end, after which vector erase removes the trailing elements.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: remove_if compacts retained elements and returns a new logical end, after which vector erase removes the trailing elements.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Algorithms may require iterator categories, sorted input, valid output capacity, or nonoverlapping ranges, and remove-like algorithms do not erase container elements themselves.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: std::remove_if directly changes a vector's size and destroys every removed element.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Replace a hand-written traversal with a clear standard algorithm and test it.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: std::remove_if directly changes a vector's size and destroys every removed element.
misconceptions.correction: remove_if rearranges and returns a new logical end; use the container's erase operation to reduce its size.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: std::remove_if directly changes a vector's size and destroys every removed element.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Standard algorithms: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
Algorithms may require iterator categories, sorted input, valid output capacity, or nonoverlapping ranges, and remove-like algorithms do not erase container elements themselves.

recap.summary: You should now be able to replace a hand-written traversal with a clear standard algorithm and test it. Keep this boundary in view: Algorithms may require iterator categories, sorted input, valid output capacity, or nonoverlapping ranges, and remove-like algorithms do not erase container elements themselves.
